summary: Gruber reviews the release of Mailsmith 2.0, a Mac OS X-only email client from Bare Bones Software, highlighting its notable features including SpamSieve integration, PGP support, and Address Book integration. He acknowledges its main limitation—lack of IMAP support—before pivoting to a broader argument about why serious email users shouldn't settle for Apple Mail just because it's free. He critiques the common practice of calling Apple's email client 'Mail.app' with a detailed aside on why filename extensions shouldn't constitute application names. His core argument is that Apple Mail tries to serve everyone and consequently serves power users poorly, and that refusing to pay for a better tool out of principle is foolish.
key_points: Mailsmith 2.0 is a solid, Mac OS X-only update with SpamSieve spam filtering, PGP, and Address Book integration, free for existing 1.5 users
The practice of calling Apple's email client 'Mail.app' is wrong—the .app suffix is filesystem metadata, not part of the application's name, and an embarrassing holdover from NeXT
Most people treat email as ephemeral and Apple Mail serves them fine, but power users who complain about it yet refuse to pay for alternatives are being stubborn
Apple Mail suffers from specific design shortcomings: mandatory top-posting, weak AppleScript support, and the gimmicky drawer interface for mailboxes
By trying to serve everyone from casual grandmothers to mailing-list power users, Apple Mail ends up too complicated for beginners and too underpowered for experts
money_quotes: If you absolutely need to use IMAP, that's unfortunate, because you're missing out on what is truly a crackerjack email client.
E.g. imagine if Safari were instead named "Web".
Using the filename as a stash for file system metadata is a terrible idea, and is an embarrassing holdover from legacy NeXT systems.
Email must be free, Apple Mail is free, thus I must use Apple Mail.
Most people aren't very serious about their email.
You're nothing more than a stubborn goof if you refuse to consider coughing up a few bucks for something better designed for your needs.
Isn't it a bit odd to expect a single email client to meet the needs of everyone from three-messages-a-week grandmothers to subscribed-to-20-mailing-lists nerds?

Attempting to be all things for all emailers, Apple Mail is stretched very thin indeed — overly complicated for beginners and those with simple needs, yet underpowered and clumsy for those who seek a precise and powerful tool.
key_insight: Free bundled software that tries to serve everyone inevitably becomes too complex for casual users and too limited for power users, and refusing to pay for a better tool out of principle is self-defeating stubbornness.
